coredata.dc:title: Microfluidic synthesis of gelatin nanoparticles conjugated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots and associated cellular response on A549 cells
coredata.dc:creator: Joseph, X.
Akhil, V.
Arathi, A.
Mohanan, P.V.
coredata.dc:description: Gelatin nanoparticles are a versatile class of nanoparticles with wide applications, especially in drug delivery and gene delivery. The inherent biocompatible nature of gelatin and various functional groups can improve the cellular interactions and enhance the efficacy of different drug formulations. Microfluidic hydrodynamic flow-focusing techniques can be used for the synthesis of gelatin nanoparticles. The present work syntheses nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated with gelatin nanoparticles (NQD-GNPs) using a microfluidic approach and associated cellular response through various assays. MTT, neutral red uptake, and Calcein AM/Propidium iodide (PI) assays independently proved the biocompatible nature of NQD-GNPs. The NQD-GNPs treatment demonstrated a slight increase in reactive nitrogen species generation and lactate dehydrogenase release. However, it does not alter the mitochondrial membrane potential or lysosomal stability. The cellular uptake of NQD-GNP depends on the concentration and does not affect the apoptotic pathway of the cells. Most of the cells remained viable even after treatment with high concentrations of NQD-GNPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microfluidics
Gelatin nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Hydrodynamic flow

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of microfluidic device: design and flow simulation studies
Polydimethylsiloxane device fabrication by replica moulding
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by microwave approach
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles by microfluidic approach
Physiochemical characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles
Assessment of cell viability by MTT assay and neutral red uptake assay
Reactive nitrogen species (RNS) generation by Griess assay
Lysosomal membrane integrity by acridine orange staining
Mitochondrial membrane potential by JC-1 staining
Live/dead analysis by Calcein AM/PI staining
Apoptosis analysis by annexin V/PI flow cytometric assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of hydrodynamic flow focusing polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) device fabrication.
Photographic images of the (i) preparation of gelatin nanoparticles conjugated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots using a microfluidic device, (ii) top view.

Hydrodynamic flow-focusing microfluidic system; (A.i) COMSOL Multiphysics simulation of the hydrodynamicflow-focusing system showing the velocity field. (A.ii). COMSOL Multiphysics simulation of the hydrodynamicflow-focusing system showing the pressure distribution (A.iii) COMSOL Multiphysics simulation of the hydrodynamicflow-focusing system showing the concentration profile (inflow-20 mM), the arrows represent the direction of flow; (B)Photograph of Hydrodynamic flow-focusing microfluidic system consisted of two inlets and one outlet, filled with colour liquid for visualisation. Inset shows the bright-field microscopic image showing the microchannels in a microfluidic device (Magnification 10x, scale bar100 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Physiochemical characterisation; (A.i) NQD after microwave-assisted synthesis; (A.ii) NQD-GNPs solution under visible light; (B) (i) Distilled Water, (ii) NQD and (iii) NQD-GNP solutions under UV light (365 nm) showing a bright blue fluorescence; (C) UV-VIS absorption spectra of NQD, Gelatin, NQD-GNPs; (D) PL spectra of NQD, Gelatin, NQD-GNPs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
TEM image of NQD (i) demonstrating the size less than 10 nm (scale bar represents 100 nm); (ii)NQD-GNP nanoparticles (scale bar 500 nm); and size distribution of GNP-NQD.
(A) FTIR spectra of Gelatin, NQD-GNP and NQD; (B) XRD spectra of NQD-GNPs demonstrating amorphous nature of nanoparticle.

Cellular response to NQD-GNPs exposure. (A) MTT assay to determine the % cell viability of A549 cells; (B) Neutral red uptake assay to determine the % cell viability of A549 cells; (C) Reactive nitrogen species (RNS) production due to the exposure of NQD-GNPs; (D) % LDH release in A549 cells on exposure to NQD-GNPs. Untreated cells were used as the negative control in all the experiments, and phenol treated cells as a positive control for MTT and NR uptake assay. The data represents the mean ± SD of representative experiments (n = 3). The asterisk denotes statistically significant differences when compared to the control group. The *represents a p-value of 0.05, and ** represents a p-value of 0.01(NC - negative control and PC- positive control). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescent microscopic images showing changes in the lysosomal stability due to the exposure of various concentrations of NQD-GNPs (magnification 20x and scale represent 20 μm).
Confocal microscopic images showing the effect of NQD-GNPs treatment in mitochondrial membrane potential (magnification 60x and scale represent 20 μm).

Cellular uptake of NQD-GNPs. (A) The histogram represents the changes in the side scattering due to the uptake of NQD-GNPs in A549 cells; Inset shows the representative image of a cell (scale bar represents 20 μm); (B) Confocal microscopic images illustrate the cellular uptake of NQD-GNP (Blue) (640 μg/mL) and also stained with JC-1, arrows represent the particle uptake, NQD-GNP (Magnification 60x, scale bar represents 20 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Live/Dead flow cytometry analysis after the treatment of various concentrations of NQD-GNPs. Insets show the representative image of dead cells and live-cell (scale bar represents 20 μm).

Apoptosis analysis of A549 cells after the treatment of various concentrations of NQD-GNPs. (A) The scatter plot of cell population in various phases of apoptosis pathway (necrotic cells (Q1), late apoptotic cells (Q2), healthy viable cells (Q3), early apoptotic (Q4). (B) (C.ii) Representative images of various populations of cells; necrotic cells (Annexin V-/PI+), late apoptotic cells (Annexin V+/PI+), healthy cells (Annexin V-/P-, and early apoptotic cells (Annexin V+/PI-) (scale bar represents 20 μm).

head.ce:title: Microfluidic synthesis of gelatin nanoparticles conjugated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots and associated cellular response on A549 cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Toxicology Division, Biomedical Technology Wing, Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology (Govt. of India), Poojapura, Trivandrum, 695 012, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology (Govt. of India)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Toxicology Division, Biomedical Technology Wing, Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology (Govt. of India), Poojapura, Trivandrum, 695 012, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gelatin nanoparticles are a versatile class of nanoparticles with wide applications, especially in drug delivery and gene delivery. The inherent biocompatible nature of gelatin and various functional groups can improve the cellular interactions and enhance the efficacy of different drug formulations. Microfluidic hydrodynamic flow-focusing techniques can be used for the synthesis of gelatin nanoparticles. The present work syntheses nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated with gelatin nanoparticles (NQD-GNPs) using a microfluidic approach and associated cellular response through various assays. MTT, neutral red uptake, and Calcein AM/Propidium iodide (PI) assays independently proved the biocompatible nature of NQD-GNPs. The NQD-GNPs treatment demonstrated a slight increase in reactive nitrogen species generation and lactate dehydrogenase release. However, it does not alter the mitochondrial membrane potential or lysosomal stability. The cellular uptake of NQD-GNP depends on the concentration and does not affect the apoptotic pathway of the cells. Most of the cells remained viable even after treatment with high concentrations of NQD-GNPs.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated with gelatin nanoparticle

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles (NPs) are particles whose size ranges in nanometers. The small size of the particles offers unique properties compared to the bulk material. Moreover, the surface of the nanoparticles can be functionalized with various molecules, including specific ligands, functional groups, polymers for the increased specificity. Gelatin nanoparticles (GNPs) are versatile, biocompatible and biodegradable with a wide range of applications, including targeted drug delivery and gene delivery. GNPs can be synthesised by different methods, including desolvation [1,2], nanoprecipitation [2–4], reverse phase microemulsion [5], emulsification [6,7] and coacervation [8,9]. However, this study focuses on a microfluidic hydrodynamic flow-focusing based technique for the preparation of GNP. The microfluidic flow-focusing preparation of nanoparticles offers narrow size distribution [10,11]. The size of the particle formed by the microfluidic approach can be tuned by controlling the fluid flow [12,13].

Microfluidics is a technology that studies and manipulates the fluid flow in micro-sized channels [14,15]. Microfluidic devices require a low volume of reagents, thereby reducing cost and environmental footprints. Hydrodynamic flow-focusing is simple and easy to operate for the production of nanoparticles [13]. The flow pattern through the microfluidic device falls into the laminar regime, where the viscous forces are prominent. The laminar flow regime offers a boundary condition dependent velocity distribution and diffusion-based mass transfer in a microchannel [16]. Generally, in a hydrodynamic flow-focusing device, an inner fluid of a lower flow rate flows within an outer sheath fluid of a high flow rate. The outer sheath fluid of a higher flow rate induces compression in central flow, reducing the mixing time. This will lead to the diffusive mass transfer in the focused stream in microchannels. The reaction is confined with the centrally focused stream rather than the channel walls, generating a homogeneous particle distribution. The reproducibility and low cost make microfluidic flow-focusing technology an attractive tool in nanoparticles production.

The main objective of the present study was to create a novel platform for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles (NQD-GNP) and to investigate the cellular response of the formed particles, including cellular uptake, cytotoxicity and the events in the cell death progression using an adenocarcinoma cell line, A549.

Ordyl SY 300 (55 μm) and Ordyl SY 300 Developer solution were purchased from Resistechno (Milan, Italy), SYLGARD™ 184 silicone elastomer kit was obtained from DOW Chemicals (Michigan, USA), and 75mmx50mm Glass slide from Corning (New York, USA).Dimethylsulfoxide(DMSO),acetone and isopropanol were purchased from Spectrochem (Mumbai, India). Dulbecco modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), and Fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco Life Technologies (New York, USA). Gelatin, Pluronics F-127 and Griess Reagent were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), Neutral Red Stain and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were procured from SRL Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, India). Acridine Orange, Antibiotic-Antimycotic solution, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and 0.25% Trypsin-EDTA were obtained from Himedia Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, India). Rhodamine Phalloidin was purchased from Abcam(Cambridge, UK). Annexin/PI apoptosis assay kit, JC-1 and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay kit were obtained from Invitrogen, Thermo Fisher Scientific (Massachusetts, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of microfluidic device: design and flow simulation studies
ce:section.ce:para: The design for the hydrodynamic flow-focusing device for nanoparticle synthesis consisted of two inlets and one outlet. It was plotted in AutoCAD software (California, USA) and saved as an"AutoCAD Drawing Interchange File (.dxf)" file. The file was then imported into the COMSOL workspace (Stockholm, Sweden) for model simulation. Prior to device fabrication, multi-physics simulations for the device were carried out using COMSOL software to obtain the flow rate for the diffusive mixing and formation of nanoparticles (Fig. 3A). Navier Stock's equation for laminar flow and transport of diluted species were simulated for the flow profile in the device. Proper inlet and outlet conditions were selected, and the fine mesh was used for the studies.

Mould fabrication was carried out using the photolithography method. The photomask for the hydrodynamic flow-focusing device was printed on a plastic film using a high-resolution printer. Subsequently, the mould was prepared by the method described by McMillan AH with some modifications [17]. Briefly, two layers of photoresist film (Ordyl SY300 Film, 55 μm) were carefully laminated (325R6, FalconK, France) onto a cleaned glass slide (sequentially then cleaned with acetone and isopropanol and dried on a hot plate, 150 °C, 5 min). The laminated glass slides were then exposed to a UV lamp (UV-KUB 2, Kloé, Montpellier, France) using a photomask. After post exposed baking (150 °C, 5 min), the mould was developed using an Ordyl SY Developer solution and cleaned with isopropanol and water followed by hard-bake at 150 °C for 30 min on a hot plate (Fischer Scientific, Pittsburgh, USA) (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polydimethylsiloxane device fabrication by replica moulding
ce:section.ce:para: Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and its curing agent accurately weighed in a ratio of 10:1 were mixed thoroughly subsequently, degassed for 30 min in a vacuum desiccator. The degassed PDMS was then poured onto the mould and cured for 2 h at 70 °C in a conventional hot air oven. After curing, the PDMS devices were peeled off from the mould, inlet and outlet holes were punched and cleaned with acetone and isopropanol. The cleaned PDMS devices and glass slides were taken and plasma-treated for 5 min under medium radio frequency (RF) power (Harrick Plasma, Ithaca, USA). The plasma-treated surfaces bonded together and were cured at 70 °C overnight for proper bonding (Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by microwave approach
ce:section.ce:para: The microwave-assisted method described by Duan et al. [18] was used to synthesise nitrogen-doped carbon dots. Briefly, citric acid (1.54 g) was dissolved in 2 mL water in a 50 mL beaker. Next, ethylenediamine (540 μL) was added, stirred for 30 min and was allowed to cool down. The solution was then placed in a domestic microwave and heated for 3 min (450 W). A brown solid was formed, and it naturally cooled down to room temperature and was dissolved in distilled water. To separate large particles, the carbon dots solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm (Centrifugation radius- 10.7 cm, Centrifuge 5804 R, Eppendorf, Germany) to separate large particles. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Finally, the solution was dried in an oven at 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles by microfluidic approach
ce:section.ce:para:
Gelatin nanoparticles were synthesised by a modified nanoprecipitation technique [4,19] using a hydrodynamic flow-focusing device. Gelatin (125 mg) was weighed and dissolved in distilled water (10 mL) at 50 °C. Later nitrogen-doped carbon dots (12.5 mg) were added and stirred for 30 min. The non-solvent phase was also prepared (4% Pluronics F-127 in acetone), and aqueous, and non-solvent phases were connected to a microfluidic pump (ElveFlow, Paris, France). The tubes were connected to the inlet/outlet of the microfluidic devices (Fig. 2
). 50 mL of non-solvent phase was taken in a beaker, and 240 μL of 25% glutaraldehyde was added. The fluid flow was started, and the flow rate was regulated by adjusting the inlet pressure using ESI software (ElveFlow, Paris, France) (Fig. 2). The diffusive mixing occurred at the flow junction, and subsequently, the nanoparticles formed were collected in the non-solvent phase containing cross-linking agent under constant stirring. The newly synthesised nanoparticles were then purified by repeated centrifugation using acetone and water. Finally, the particles were dissolved in distilled water and freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The particles size was analysed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). UV-absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were also analysed to check the absorbance and fluorescence of the NQD-GNPs. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out to find the functional groups present in the nanoparticle and its crystalline structure.

The cellular effects of carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles were studied on Adenocarcinoma human alveolar basal epithelial cells, A549 (National Centre for Cell Science, Pune, India). Cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic solution at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The seeding densities of the cells were selected according to the assays performed (1 × 104 cells/well for colourimetric assays, 1 × 105 cells/well for staining assays and 1 × 106 cells/well for flow cytometry analysis). After 24hoursof cell seeding, the growth media was withdrawn and replaced with media containing varying doses of NQD for all assays. 1 mg/mL stock solutions of NQD-GNPs were prepared in DMEM, and dilutions were performed in media to obtain the different concentrations. Untreated cells were used as the negative control (NC) in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of cell viability by MTT assay and neutral red uptake assay
ce:section.ce:para: A colourimetric assay with MTT was used to study the cytotoxicity effects of NQD-GNPs in A549 cells [20]. MTT (5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 3 h after the treatment with NQD-GNPs (40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL). To solubilise the formazan crystals formed due to the reduction of tetrazolium salt, DMSO was added. A multi-well plate reader (Bio-Tek Instruments, USA) was used to measure the absorbance at 540 nm. Phenol treated cells were used as the positive control. The percentage of cell viability was calculated using the relative absorbance of various concentrations of NQD-GNPs treated cells with respect to the control cells.

The effect of NQD-GNPs exposure on the lysosomal activity of A549 was assessed by neutral red uptake (NRU) assay [21]. Following the treatment of different concentrations of NQD-GNPs for 24 h (40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL), 0.1% of neutral red solution was added and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C, 5%CO2. The neutral red solution was removed and washed twice with PBS. The dye entrapped inside the cell were solubilised by the incubation of cells with neutral red desorbing reagent consisted of glacial acetic acid, 95% ethanol, deionised water in the ratio of 1:50:49 with gentle shaking for 15 min. Absorbance was measured at 540 nm using a microplate reader (ELx 808, BioTek Instruments,USA). The percentage cell viability was calculated for each concentration of NQD-GNP with respect to the untreated cells (negative control). Phenol treated cells were used as the positive control for the cell viability assay.

The release of LDH into the cell culture medium was used to assess cellular cytotoxicity [22]. The assay quantitatively measures the extracellular LDH released into the culture medium upon any damage caused to the plasma membrane by NQD-GNPs. LDH leakage assay was performed by using the CyQUANT™ LDH Cytotoxicity Assay kit (Invitrogen). After treatment with various concentrations of NQD-GNPs (40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL), the cell culture media was transferred to a new plate, and the reaction mixture was added according to the manufacture's guidelines. After 30minutesof incubation at room temperature, the assays were arrested by adding the stop solution, and absorbance was measured at 490 nm and 680 nm using a microplate reader (Synergy H1 Hybrid, BioTek Instruments, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive nitrogen species (RNS) generation by Griess assay
ce:section.ce:para: Griess Reagent was used to study the effect of reactive nitrogen species generation in the cytotoxicity to NQD-GNPs exposed A549 cells by a spectrophotometric analysis [23]. Following the treatment with NQD-GNPs for 24 h (40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL), the supernatant was collected and mixed with an equal volume of Griess reagent. After incubation for 25 min in the dark, absorbance was measured at 540 nm (ELx 808, BioTek Instruments, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosomal membrane integrity by acridine orange staining
ce:section.ce:para: Acridine orange (AO) staining was used to analyse the changes in lysosomal membrane integrity of A549 cells due to the exposure of NQD-GNPs [24,25]. In acidic pH (acid vesicles), AO exhibits red fluorescence; however, AO exhibits green fluorescence in less acidic pH (cytoplasm). Hence the cells with intact lysosomes exhibit red fluorescence. The cells exposed to NQD-GNPs (160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL) were stained with 5 μg/mL of AO for 30 min. The cells were washed with PBS and were observed under a fluorescence microscope using a green and a red filter(Axio Scope.A1, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mitochondrial membrane potential by JC-1 staining
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in the mitochondrial potential due to the exposure of NQD-GNPs were assessed by tetraethyl benzimidazolylcarbocyanine iodide (JC-1) staining. After the exposure of various concentrations of NQD-GNPs (160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL) for 24 h, cells were washed with PBS and stained with JC-1(2 μM) and incubated for 30 min. Finally, PBS wash was given and observed using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (Olympus IX83, Japan). The NQD-GNPs uptake was also analysed by CLSM by setting an emission at 450–470 nm.

Imaging flow cytometry was performed to evaluate the uptake of NQD-GNPs [26]. The intensity of the side scatter was analysed(FlowSight®, Amnis, USA). Single-cell suspensions of NQD-GNPs (160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL) exposed cells were prepared. 488 nm laser illuminated the cells in the sample stream. The scatter plot of forward scatter (FSC) versus the side scatter (SSC), and the histogram of SSC versus normalized frequency analysed the particle uptake. The analysis was performed in the IDEAS® software (Amnis®, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live/dead analysis by Calcein AM/PI staining
ce:section.ce:para: Calceinacetoxymethyl ester (Calcein AM)/PI staining was performed to study the cellular viability of NQD-GNPs exposed A549 cells. The intracellular esterase activity in live cells converts Calcein AM (non-fluorescent) to Calcein (fluorescent). PI enters the dead cells and thereby intercalates with the nucleic acids giving out red fluorescence. Briefly, the cells were harvested after treating with various concentrations of NQD-GNPs (160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL) and stained with 1 μg/mL of Calcein AM, incubated for 45 min in the dark. The cells were again washed with PBS and stained with PI of a concentration of 2.5 μM for 5 min in the dark and analysed by flow cytometry (FlowSight®, Amnis, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apoptosis analysis by annexin V/PI flow cytometric assay
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of cell death in cells treated with NQD-GNPs was analysed by Annexin/PI flow cytometric assay [27]. The staining was carried out following the manufacturer's guidelines (Alexa Fluor® 488 annexin-V/PI detection kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific)). The cells exposed to various concentrations (160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL, 640 μg/mL) of NQD-GNPs were harvested and incubated with Alexa Fluor® 488 annexin V/PI in annexin binding buffer 15 min. Annexin V staining was detected as green fluorescence and PI as red fluorescence while analyzing using imaging flow cytometry (FlowSight®, Amnis, USA). Untreated cells were used as the negative control. Individually stained cells (PI alone, Annexin V alone) and unstained cells were used to carry out compensation. Further analysis was performed using IDEAS® software (Amnis®, USA).

The experiments such as MTT, NR Uptake, LDH leakage and RNS production were performed in triplicates, and the results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. The statistical analysis was carried out using the student's t-test. The significance level was calculated and represented as a p-value. The p-values 0.05, 0.01 and 0.001 represented by *, ** and *** respectively.

NQD-GNPs were prepared using a microfluidic flow-focusing approach. The microfluidic devices (Fig. 3B) with a channel thickness of 110 μm were successfully fabricated using photolithography and replica moulding. The COMSOL multi-physics software simulation using Navier Stock's equation demonstrated diffusive mixing at the junction of channels (Fig. 3). Subsequently, the same inlet conditions were used for the experiments.

NQDs were initially prepared by microwave heating (Fig. 4 A i). Then the NQD-GNPs were synthesised by nanoprecipitation in hydrodynamic flow-focusing microfluidic devices (Figure 3B). The NQD was coupled to gelatin via carboxylic groups of NQD of NQD and amino groups of gelatin. The nanoparticles were obtained through nanoprecipitation. NQD-GNPs were formed in the flow-focusing stream and mixed in the organic solvent containing the cross-linker.

The physicochemical characterization was carried out using a variety of techniques. The brown solid (Fig. 4A i) was obtained after the microwave heating was purified and utilized for the synthesis of NQD-GNP. The NQDs prepared by microwave heating and the NQD-GNPs prepared through the microfluidic approach were visualised under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 4B) and exhibited a bright blue fluorescence confirming its conjugation with nitrogen-doped carbon dots. However, the intensity of NQD-GNP (Fig. 4B iii) was less compared to the NQD (Fig. 4B ii). The UV–Vis spectra of NQD-GNP demonstrated a broad peak at 350 nm and one at 250 nm (Fig. 4C). The PL emission spectra of NQD-GNP, when excited at 360 nm, demonstrated a peak at 450 nm (Fig. 4D). The absorbance and PL emission intensity of the NQD-GNPs were found to be less than the NQD of similar concentration owing to the conjugation. However, there was no shift in the absorbance or fluorescence wavelength.

To study the biocompatibility of synthesised NQD-GNPs, MTT assay were performed after treating A549 (adenocarcinoma) cells with NQD-GNPs (Fig. 7 A). The cell viability assay demonstrated a concentration-dependent decrease in cell viability. However, the cell viability was greater than 50% in the maximum concentration of the particle (640 μg/mL). The cell viabilities were 87.66 ± 8.43, 79.54 ± 11.68, 79.63 ± 10.05, 79.63 ± 11.63, 66.26 ± 7.80 (p < 0.01) for the NQD-GNPs concentration of 40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL and 640 μg/mL respectively.

Neutral red uptake assay was also performed to validate the biocompatibility of NQD-GNPs (Fig. 7B). The percentage cell viabilities of NQD-GNPs obtained from the NR uptake assay was 97.58 ± 15.15, 89.87 ± 14.74, 91.49 ± 7.95, 91.34 ± 18.18, 97.18 ± 12.15 for 40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL and 640 μg/mL concentrations of NQD-GNP.

Nitric oxide release associated with the cellular exposure of NQD-GNP of various concentrations was estimated by the Modified Griess reagent test (Fig. 7C). The purple azo product formed through the reaction of nitrite in the sample to the Griess reagent was obtained by measuring the absorbance at 540 nm. The NQD- GNPs exposure resulted in the generation of nitric oxide in a concentration-dependent manner. The absorbance at 540 nm was 0.084667 ± 0.005033, 0.087 ± 0.004359, 0.088333 ± 0.000577, 0.09 ± 0.004359 (p < 0.05) and 0.086 ± 0.006245 for 40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL and 640 μg/mL of NQD-GNPs.

The A549 cellular exposure of NQD-GNP resulted in a concentration-dependent increase of LDH leakage from the cell. A significant percentage of leakage of LDH was observed in cells exposed to various concentrations of NQD-GNP (Fig. 7D). The percentage LDH leakage was 107.1816 ± 0.846206 (p < 0.05), 107.5881 ± 1.023013 (p < 0.05), 112.3306 ± 1.24189 (p < 0.05), 113.0081 ± 3.174898 (p < 0.01) and 116.9377 ± 13.06095 for cells treated with NQD-GNP of concentration of 40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL and 640 μg/mL with respect to the control cells.

To visualise the lysosomal integrity and stability, acridine orange (AO) stain was used. The AO dye can penetrate the lysosome and retain red fluorescence due to the acidic environment. The NQD-GNPs treatment in A549 did not change the lysosome stability up to a 640 μg/mL (Fig. 8 ).

Mitochondrial membrane potential reflects mitochondrial activity and cell health. The effect of NQD-GNPs in mitochondrial membrane potential was studied by JC-1 staining. JC-1 can accumulate in mitochondria of healthy cells and forms J-aggregates. However, the NQD-GNP particle treatment did not significantly affect the mitochondrial membrane potential of the A549 cells (Fig. 9 ).

The cellular uptake of NQD-GNPs was analysed using flow cytometry by measuring the changes in the SSC and FSC. The single cells population was gated in a scatter plot of FSC versus SSC. The internalisation of nanoparticles increased the granularity of the cells and increased the mean intensity of the side scatter. The mean intensity was 153.56 ± 61.04, 165.01 ± 60.43, 177.71 ± 62.43 and 244.05 ± 59.23 for 40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL, 160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL and 640 μg/mL of NQD-GNPs (Fig. 10 A).

The NQD-GNPs demonstrated the emission peak centered at 450 nm while exciting with 360 nm UV light. Therefore, the cellular uptake of NQD-GNP was analysed by confocal microscopy with emission wavelengths between 450 and 479 nm. The nanoparticles uptake in cells treated with NQD-GNP were observed as blue fluorescence. The nanoparticles were found to be dispersed in the cytoplasm (Fig. 10B).

The quantitative live/dead analysis was done using Calcein-AM/PI double staining. Calcein AM stains only the viable cells, and the PI stains the dead cells. PI only passes through the compromised cell membrane, not through the viable cell membrane. The live/dead analysis using Calcein-AM/PI double staining demonstrated almost 90% of viable cells in a concentration of up to 640 μg/mL of NQD-GNPs (Fig. 11 ).

The effect of NQD-GNPs in apoptosis was evaluated by Annexin-V FITC/PI dual staining (Fig. 7). The early apoptotic cells were characterized by annexin +/PI-, late apoptotic cells by annexin+/PI+, dead cells by annexin-/PI+ and the normal cells by annexin-/PI- (Fig. 12 ).The scatter plot demonstrated that most cells were located in the 1st quadrant (annexin-V FITC-/PI-), indicating the live cells. The NQD-GNPs treatment slightly increased cells in the early apoptotic phase (Annexin V-FITC+/PI-).

Microfluidic devices control and manipulate fluid flow in a geometrically constrained channel of size in the micrometer scale. Microfluidic device's inherent properties include the low reagent requirement, high surface to volume ratio, rapid heat and mass transfer, accurate control over the reaction conditions, and safer operation strategies than macroscale make them an optimum tool in nanoparticle preparation. The present study focuses on applying microfluidic devices to synthesis nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated gelatin nanoparticles. A hydrodynamic flow-focusing device was designed using AutoCAD software, fluid flow simulated in COMSOL Multiphysics software to determine the optimum flow rate of reagents for nanoparticle production. Soft lithography and replica moulding were used for the microfluidic device fabrication.

Hydrodynamic flow-focusing is advantageous in producing monodisperse and small size nanoparticles compared to the bulk formulation, which is already reported in the literature. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots were prepared, characterised, and stirred with gelatin solution before nanoparticle production. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots-gelatin solution in water consisted of an aqueous phase pumped through the central horizontal channel (Inlet 2) and the organic phase consisted of poloxamer F-127 in acetone is pumped through the side channels (Inlet 1) (Fig. 3B). The diffusion of the organic phase into the aqueous phase reduced gelatin's solubility, leading to the nanoprecipitation of gelatin nanoparticles (Fig. 3A iii). The newly formed NQD-GNPs were then cross-linked with glutaraldehyde. The NQD-GNPs were purified and collected by repeated centrifugation using acetone and finally with the distilled water and lyophilised.

The physicochemical characterisation of the newly synthesised particle was carried out. UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence of NQD-GNPs were analysed and compared with the NQDs and gelatin. NQD and NQD-GNPs exhibited similar absorption wavelengths, but the absorbance intensity was less in NQD-GNPs than in the NQDs (Fig. 4C). This may be attributed to the possible reaction between NQDs and GNPs. The absorption spectra of NQD-GNPs showed peaks at 350 nm and 250 nm indicating n- π* transition of CO and π- π* transition of sp2 domains. The fluorescence spectral studies revealed a strong emission of NQDs and NQD-GNPs centered at 450 nm when excited with 360 nm (Fig. 4D). The stretching vibrations of various functional groups and bonds present in NQD-GNPs were analysed by FTIR and compared with FTIR spectra of NQDs and gelatin. The peaks in the spectra of NQD-GNPs at 3302 cm−1and 1639.49 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of O–H and CC bonds. The CHN (aldimine) linkage due to glutaraldehyde cross-linking was observed at 1448.54 cm−1. Similarly, the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds were observed at 2935.66 cm−1, the peak at 1332 cm−1 contributing to the vibration of C–N. The symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching of C–O–C groups was observed at 796.6 cm−1and 1082.07 cm−1 [28]. For NQD, the broad peak at 3309.85 cm−1 represents the combined stretching vibration of the N–H and O–H bond (Fig. 6A). Overall, the FTIR studies illustrated the formation of NQD, NQD-GNP and the cross-linking by glutaraldehyde.

The broad peaks observed in the XRD pattern of NQD-GNP around 2Ɵ = 22.5 shows poor crystallinity (amorphous nature) of the NQD conjugated GNPs (Fig. 6B). The obtained XRD pattern is in accordance with the previously reported XRD data in the literature Click or tap here to enter text.The TEM image shows the formation of amorphous particles without any aggregation, and the size of NQDs was less than 10 nm, and NQD-GNPs were 25.30 ± 6.68 nm (Fig. 5). Previous reports also suggested the amorphous nature of Gelatin nanoparticles [1]. Altogether, there will not be any characteristic peaks in the XRD data due to the amorphous nature of the NQD conjugated Gelatin nanoparticles. The X-ray diffraction analysis experimental data confirms this with no extra peaks other than the gelatin nanoparticle characteristic peak in the spectrum.

The gelatin's biodegradable and biocompatible nature makes it a suitable raw material for nanoparticle synthesis for biomedical applications. Gelatin nanoparticles can be used for gene and drug delivery [29]. Various functional groups present in the gelatin allow the conjugation of different molecules, and more importantly, the coating with gelatin can reduce the toxicity associated with various molecules [30,31]. Therefore the present study demonstrates the microfluidic synthesis of NQD-GNP and its cellular response in the A549 cells. A549 is an alveolar epithelial cancer cell line widely employed to model pulmonary uptake and toxicity associated with the nanoparticles [32]. Gelatin based nanoparticle synthesis has been well established. However, most methods are batch processes, often time-consuming and produce nanoparticles with larger size distribution. The microfluidic assisted method is a facile, continuous process and offers a smaller size compared to the conventional synthesis process. Out of different methods described for drug loading into gelatin nanoparticles, dissolving the drug in gelatin solution before the nanoparticle formation offers maximum loading efficiency [33]. Hence NQD were mixed with gelatin solution prior to the nanoparticle formation.

The protein nanoparticles, such as gelatin nanoparticles, generally follow a biphasic drug release pattern consist of immediate release of weakly associated surface bound drug molecules and slow diffusion of tightly bonded molecules from the matrix [34]. The smaller particle can exhibit a rapid release of drug molecules than the larger particle. GNPs can offer targeted drug delivery and controlled drug release, and they can also provide passive drug targeting through enhanced permeability and retention effect in tumour regions. The paclitaxel encapsulation using gelatin nanoparticles increased the drug release and enhanced solubility (Lu Z 2004). The anti-cancer drug-loaded gelatin has demonstrated a better anti-cancer effect than free drugs due to increased endocytotic uptake [35–37]. It has been reported that GNPs showed a prolonged drug release profile, decreased the side effect associated with the toxic drug molecules and improved the bioavailability. In this study, the biocompatible nature of NQD-GNPs was analysed by the common the cell viability assays such as MTT and NR uptake assay. The cell viability assay revealed more than 60% cell viability at a higher concentration on exposure to GNP-NQDs (640 μg/mL). MTT assay evaluates the ability of the succinate-dehydrogenase enzyme of viable cells to convert MTT to the blue formazan product [20]. However, neutral red uptake assay quantifies the viable cells based on the ability to incorporate the supravital dye, neutral red, in the lysosome [21]. The cell viability in the NRU assay was found to be more when compared to MTT at a higher concentration (640 μg/mL) of NQD-GNPs (Fig. 7A&B). The result obtained from the cytotoxicity assays demonstrates that the cell death mechanism involves the interaction of NQD-GNP with the mitochondrial compartment and/or lysosomal compartment could be affected in the later stage [38].

Many physiological conditions are mediated by nitric oxide (NO). Hence, the measurement of NO is an essential tool for accessing pathological conditions. Nevertheless, because of its short half-life, it is not easy to quantify. However, the NO generation can be indirectly quantified by end degradation products such as nitrite and nitrate. The nanoparticle treatment can increase stress on cells, which can be evident from the generation of reactive oxygen species and reactive nitrogen species. Even though the cell death was minimum at a higher concentration, the treatment of NQD-GNPs resulted in a concentration-dependent release of RNS (Fig. 7C). The generation of RNS may result in their interaction with the various proteins, lipids, cellular membrane, and subcellular organelles membrane, leading to cellular damage [39].

On the other hand, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is a stable cytoplasmic enzyme inside the cells. However, the damaged or compromised cellular membrane will lead to the release of LDH. LDH leakage is a suitable parameter for analyzing the degree of plasma membrane damage in a cell population [22]; also, it is a cellular marker of apoptosis. The NQD-GNP has resulted in a slight increase in LDH leakage from the cell (Fig. 7D). The higher concentration (640 μg/mL) of NQD-GNPs resulted in a 16% increase of LDH release than the untreated cells. It suggested that at higher concentrations, the NQD-GNPs can induce stress on the cells and alter the cellular membrane integrity.

The NQD-GNPs, upon cell entry, can be targeted towards the lysosome. The lysosome is a membranous vesicle, and it involves in the macromolecule's digestion [24]. The cytoplasmic pH is slightly alkaline, but the lysosome pH is acidic. The possible alteration in the lysosome functions was studied using AO staining. The viable cellular lysosome can accumulate acridine orange through an ATP-dependent proton pump present in the lysosomal membrane [25]. The accumulation of AO can be seen in the early stages of apoptosis; however, not in the necrosis due to leaky or ruptured lysosome [40]. In this present study, lysosome stability was investigated by acridine orange staining. The treatment of NQD-GNPs up to a concentration of 640 μg/mL did not alter the red fluorescence of AO significantly. Thus, the nanoparticle treatment did not affect the lysosome integrity and stability consistent with the neutral red uptake assay (Fig. 8). Similarly, the mitochondrial membrane potential of A549 treated with various concentrations of NQD-GNPs was analysed by JC-1 staining. The NQD-GNPs treatment did not produce any significant change in the fluorescence of JC-1 dimers (present in the active mitochondria) (Fig. 9).

Cellular uptake of NQD-GNPs depends on the concentration of nanoparticles. At the higher concentration, the particle uptake was high and vice versa. The uptake of NQD-GNP was studied by flow cytometry by measuring the scattering of the laser light (Fig. 10A). The cell size or surface area can be correlated with the forward scattered light, whereas the side scattered light is proportional to cellular granularity [41]. NQD-GNPs internalisation increased the complexity of the cells, which can be attributed to the increase in the SSC intensity [42].The particle uptake was also visualised by using CLSM with an emission wavelength of 450–470 nm. The spectrofluorometric studies were demonstrated the fluorescence nature of NQD-GNPs. The synthesised NQD-GNPs exhibited an emission peak of 450 nm while excited with a 360 nm laser (Fig. 4D). The NQD-GNPs up taken by cells are therefore visualised as blue fluorescence while exciting with the 405 nm laser (Fig. 10B). The particles were observed as dispersed in the cytoplasm.

The effect of NQD-GNPs on intracellular esterase activity was studied using Calcein-AM/PI staining. Calcein-AM is a lipophilic and cell membrane permeable molecule [43]. The esterase in the viable cells converts the Calcein-AM to Calcein, giving bright green fluorescence [44]. Alternatively, PI cannot pass through the cell membrane of viable cells, and it only passes through the damaged or disordered cell membrane and stains the nucleus. The treatment with the NQD-GNPs showed no difference in the intracellular esterase activity compared to the untreated group. The number of live cells was almost similar in all the test samples (160 μg/mL, 320 μg/mL and 640 μg/mL of NQD-GNP) (Fig. 11).

The cell death pathway associated with NQD-GNPs treatment in the A549 cell line was analysed by Annexin V-FITC/PI-based flow cytometry assay. The data from the experiment demonstrated that most of the cells were in a viable state after the treatment with NQD-GNPs; similarly, NQD-GNPs treatment slightly increased the number of cells in the late apoptotic stage (Annexin V+/PI+) than untreated cells and also slightly increased the number of cells in the early apoptotic stage (Annexin V+/PI-) but not significantly (Fig. 12). Overall the NQD-GNPs treatment did not significantly influence the cell viability of A549 cells.

NQD-GNPs were successfully synthesised using a hydrodynamic flow-focusing microfluidic device. The device fabrication was carried out using photolithography and replica moulding. The synthesised nanoparticles were amorphous, and it was further characterised by various techniques such as UV–Vis spectroscopy, spectrofluorometry, TEM and FTIR. The fluorescence of NQD-GNPs was less when compared to pristine NQDs, which may be attributed to the conjugation of NQD to the GNPs. Even though the treatment of NQD-GNPs to the A549 cells resulted in a concentration-dependent reduction of cell viability, but also maintained cellular viability more than 60% in the highest concentration tested (640 μg/mL). The exposure of NQD-GNPs does not significantly alter the cellular properties and functions of A549 cells. The present study demonstrated a facile method of synthesis of gelatin nanoparticles using a microfluidic device and showed that this process could be used for drug conjugation. Additionally, this approach offers a reduced size compared to the bulk preparation methods.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request. Some data may not be made available because of privacy or ethical restrictions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
No funds received from other sources.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their sincere thanks to Director and Head, Biomedical Technology Wing, Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology (Govt. of India), Trivandrum, for their support and for providing the infrastructure to carry out this work. AA, JX, AV and PVM thank the Department of Science and Technology, Govt. of India, New Delhi for financial support (DST/TDT/DDP-04/2018(G).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanism of cellular uptake and impact of ferucarbotran on macrophage physiology
PLoS One

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-Y. Yang, M.-F. Tai, C.-P. Lin, C.-W. Lu, J.-L. Wang, J.-K. Hsiao, H.-M. Liu, Mechanism of cellular uptake and impact of ferucarbotran on macrophage physiology, PloS One. 6 (2011) e25524-e25524. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0025524.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving the flow cytometry-based detection of the cellular uptake of gold nanoparticles
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wu, M.R.K. Ali, K. Dansby, M.A. El-Sayed, Improving the Flow Cytometry-based Detection of the Cellular Uptake of Gold Nanoparticles., Analytical Chemistry. 91 (2019) 14261-14267. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.9b02248.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison of annexin V and calcein-AM as early vital markers of apoptosis in adherent cells by confocal laser microscopy
J. Histochem. Cytochem. : Official Journal of the Histochemistry Society

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Gatti, S. Belletti, G. Orlandini, O. Bussolati, V. Dall'Asta, G.C. Gazzola, Comparison of annexin V and calcein-AM as early vital markers of apoptosis in adherent cells by confocal laser microscopy., The Journal of Histochemistry and Cytochemistry : Official Journal of the Histochemistry Society. 46 (1998) 895-900. https://doi.org/10.1177/002215549804600804.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cell-based assays using calceinacetoxymethyl ester show variation in fluorescence with treatment conditions
Journal of Biological Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.L. Miles, J.E. Lynch, R.A. Sikes, Cell-based assays using calceinacetoxymethyl ester show variation in fluorescence with treatment conditions, Journal of Biological Methods. 2 (2015) e29. https://doi.org/10.14440/jbm.2015.73.
